source.definition: To say something nice or appreciative to someone.

explanation.sourceText: The English word 'compliment' comes from the French 'compliment', which itself derives from the Latin 'complere' meaning 'to fill up' or 'complete'. It was adopted in the 17th century as a polite expression.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „compliment” provine din francezul 'compliment', care la rândul său derivă din latină 'complere' (a completa, a umple) și a fost preluat în secolul al XVII-lea ca expresie politicoasă.

target.definition: A spune cuiva ceva frumos sau apreciativ.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian word 'compliment' was borrowed directly from the French 'compliment', which in turn comes from the Latin 'complere'.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul românesc 'compliment' este împrumutat direct din francezul 'compliment', care la rândul său provine din latinul 'complere'.

examples.sourceText: I make you a compliment on your new dress.
examples.targetText: Îți fac un compliment pentru rochia ta nouă.

examples.sourceText: She made him a compliment on his work.
examples.targetText: I-a făcut un compliment pentru munca lui.

examples.sourceText: It is nice to receive a compliment.
examples.targetText: Este frumos să primești un compliment.

examples.sourceText: One should make compliments more often.
examples.targetText: Ar trebui să faci mai des complimente.

learningTips.text: The Romanian word 'compliment' sounds almost the same as the English 'compliment' – this makes it easy to remember.
The English word 'compliment' comes from Latin 'complere', meaning 'to fill up' – think of a compliment as something that fills someone's heart with joy.
In English we say 'make a compliment', and in Romanian 'a face un compliment' – both verbs mean 'to make' or 'a face'.
Imagine you 'make' (a face) someone a 'compliment' flower – this can help you remember the phrase 'a face un compliment'.
